ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Indomie Bakso Penyet. FOODON_00002473 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FKP (noodle-based dish) is the closest defensible parent. No specific code exists for Indomie Bakso Penyet in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific dish or its immediate parent category.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Indomie Bakso Penyet is approximately 380 g, comprising a portion of cooked instant noodles (~200 g), two to three smashed meatballs (~120 g), and accompanying sauce and garnish (~60 g).

nutrient_source.title: Instant noodles, cooked (Indomie-type) with beef meatballs — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Noodles, Chinese, chow mein, dry, USDA #20113) and USDA FoodData Central Branded Foods (Indomie Mi Goreng instant noodles) combined with FAO/INFOODS SEA food composition data for bakso (beef meatball)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Indomie Bakso Penyet as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked instant wheat noodles (energy ~138 kcal/100 g cooked), beef meatball (bakso) data from FAO/INFOODS SEA (energy ~172 kcal/100 g), and seasoning/sauce components, then weighted by typical serving composition (cooked noodles ~53%, meatballs ~32%, sauce/garnish ~15%) to yield a blended per-100 g profile. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, boiled (analogous estimate for instant wheat noodles)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indomie Bakso Penyet. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled wheat noodles / instant noodles (GI range 47–58, mean ~53), which is the closest defensible analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (53 × 70.3) / 100 ≈ 37.3, where available carbohydrate per 380 g serving = 18.5 × 3.8 = 70.3 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg/100 g; ~1976 mg per serving) from instant noodle seasoning sachets and added sauces
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Indomie Bakso Penyet due to its very high sodium load (~1976 mg per 380 g serving, approximately 86% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg), primarily contributed by the instant noodle seasoning sachet and sweet soy sauce; reducing sauce quantity or choosing lower-sodium seasoning options can partially mitigate risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (~53) and high glycemic load per serving (~37.3) due to refined wheat noodle base
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume Indomie Bakso Penyet in moderation; while the GI is in the low-to-moderate range (~53), the large carbohydrate load per serving (~70 g available carbohydrate) produces a high glycemic load (~37.3), which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation, and portion reduction or substitution with lower-GI noodles is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from meatballs and seasoning oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Indomie Bakso Penyet poses moderate concern due to its high sodium content (~1976 mg/serving) and moderate saturated fat (~7.2 g/serving), both of which are associated with elevated blood pressure and atherogenic risk; consumption should be infrequent and accompanied by increased vegetable intake to improve the overall dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Indomie Bakso Penyet is a popular Singaporean-Indonesian street food dish consisting of Indomie brand instant wheat noodles served with bakso penyet — Indonesian-style beef or chicken meatballs that have been smashed (penyet means 'smashed' in Javanese/Indonesian) to create a flattened, slightly crispy exterior while retaining a juicy interior. The dish is typically assembled by boiling the Indomie noodles with their seasoning sachet, then topping them with one or more smashed meatballs, drizzling with sweet soy sauce (kecap manis) and sambal chilli, and garnishing with fried shallots. The flavour profile is savoury, slightly sweet, spicy, and umami-rich, with a soft noodle texture contrasting the firmer meatball. In Singapore, this dish is served at Indonesian-concept casual dining restaurants such as Dapur Penyet and is priced around SGD 10–11. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~148 kcal/100 g, ~562 kcal per 380 g serving) with a reasonable protein contribution from the meatballs (~7.8 g/100 g) and a high carbohydrate load from the refined wheat noodles (~18.5 g/100 g). The sodium content is clinically significant (~520 mg/100 g; ~1976 mg/serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low-moderate), but the glycemic load per serving is high (~37.3), which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains gluten and soy allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Indomie Bakso Penyet?

Where to eat Indomie Bakso Penyet in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Indomie Bakso Penyet as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted combination of USDA FoodData Central data for cooked instant wheat noodles and FAO/INFOODS SEA data for beef meatball (bakso), weighted by typical serving composition. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the estimation methodology is transparent.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be verified against a laboratory analysis or HPB Singapore Foodo entry if one becomes available. 2. Vitamin A (vitamin_a_mcg_rae) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases used for this estimate. 3. Vitamin B6 (vitamin_b6_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA bakso entry and is not reliably available for the instant noodle component. 4. Vitamin B12 (vitamin_b12_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary component sources. 5. Folate (folate_mcg_dfe) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. 6. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. 7. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. 8. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases. 9. The cuisine field is set to 'Singapore' per the input record, reflecting the dish's context as served in Singapore; the region of origin is noted as Indonesia/Singapore to capture the Indonesian culinary heritage. 10. Halal status is marked true based on the restaurant context (Dapur Penyet, a halal-certified Indonesian restaurant chain in Singapore); this should be verified per outlet. 11. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled wheat noodles; no direct measurement for this composite dish exists in approved sources.
